facts_disclosed: Looking at local CDL jobs in Atlanta — tired of over-the-road

turns.content: hey so i been thinking about something a lot lately and i want to get your take on it because i dont really have anyone to talk to about this stuff at 11pm in a truck stop parking lot in ohio
I'm here. What's on your mind?

so i been looking at local driving jobs in atlanta like city delivery or yard work stuff where you go home every single night and sleep in your own bed and i pulled up indeed last night and there are a bunch of them paying decent not as much as over the road but decent
That's a big change from long-haul. What's driving you to think about going local? Is it a specific thing or more of a general feeling?

its everything man like this over the road thing is lonely for real like really truly lonely i eat every single meal alone at some truck stop or fast food place and yeah i talk to people here and there but its not the same as having actual people in your life you know actual daily contact with humans who know you
That kind of isolation wears on you over time. Eating alone every night, no real social connection day to day -- that's not a small thing. How long have you been feeling this way?

honestly its been building for a while but it really hit me when i missed naomis birthday in september i was stuck in pennsylvania with a load that got delayed and i couldnt make it to newark in time and she was so cool about it on facetime she was like its okay daddy but i could see in her face that it wasnt okay and that broke me man that really broke me

Kids say it's okay because they love you, but they feel it. And you feel it too. Missing milestones like birthdays hits different when you know it wasn't your choice -- it was the job keeping you away.

exactly and thanksgiving is coming up and im already looking at my schedule and i dont think i can make it again because november is busy season and dispatch is stacking loads and i been doing this for years now missing holidays missing birthdays missing everything and for what you know to drive a truck back and forth across the country hauling stuff for other people while my daughter grows up without me there

That's a heavy realization. The trade-off made sense at some point -- the pay, the miles, the freedom -- but it sounds like the cost is catching up with you. What kind of local jobs are you seeing in Atlanta?

theres city delivery jobs like delivering to grocery stores and restaurants in the metro area and theres yard jockey work moving trailers around at distribution centers and some of them pay like 55 60 thousand a year which is less than im making now but you go home every night you get weekends off you get holidays off and maybe i could actually have a life you know like go to church on sunday or have dinner with my cousin marc or just be in my apartment like a normal person

The pay cut is real, but so is everything you'd be gaining. Having evenings and weekends, being able to build a routine, being there for holidays and birthdays -- that's hard to put a dollar amount on. Would 55-60K work with your expenses and the money you send for Naomi?

it would be tight but my studio apartment is only like 800 a month and i dont have expensive habits i mean i eat truck stop food which is cheap and bad but if i was home i could actually cook which would save money and be healthier too because my cholesterol situation is not getting better eating at loves and pilot every night thats for sure

That's a good point -- being home would give you control over your diet, which could make a real difference for your health. And you're right, truck stop food every night is not doing your cholesterol any favors. Have you applied to any of these local jobs or are you still in the browsing phase?

still browsing because part of me keeps going back and forth like some nights i feel like this and other nights im driving through the mountains with the music playing and i think maybe this isnt so bad but then i wake up in the morning stiff and sore and i realize my knees hurt and my back hurts and im only 35 years old and i think this cant be the rest of my life you know this cant be what the next 30 years look like

That physical toll is important to factor in. Long-haul driving is hard on the body -- the sitting, the lack of movement, the poor eating options. At 35, you still have time to make a change before it becomes permanent damage. But going back and forth is natural for a decision this big.

yeah and i was listening to a podcast about sunk cost fallacy the other day and i think thats what keeps me stuck like ive been doing this for twelve years so i feel like i have to keep doing it but thats not a reason right just because you been doing something for a long time doesnt mean you have to keep doing it especially if its costing you the things that actually matter

That's exactly what sunk cost fallacy is -- feeling like you have to keep going because of what you've already invested. But twelve years of experience isn't wasted if you go local. Your CDL, your knowledge, your driving skills -- all of that transfers. You'd just be applying it in a way that lets you have a life outside the cab.

mwen fatige man im just tired not like sleepy tired but tired in my soul you know like i sat in the truck tonight after i parked and i just stared at the steering wheel for like twenty minutes thinking about everything and i think im going to start applying to some of those local jobs when i get back to atlanta next week not quitting tomorrow but just seeing whats out there sa k pase thats where im at right now

There's nothing wrong with exploring your options. Applying and seeing what's out there doesn't commit you to anything -- it just gives you information so when you're ready to decide, you're deciding with real offers in front of you instead of hypotheticals. Get some rest tonight and take care of yourself. You don't have to figure it all out at 11pm in Ohio.
